summary: The post examines the apparent contradiction in Silicon Valley's reactions to two concurrent issues: Apple's refusal to build an FBI backdoor and India's ban on Facebook's Free Basics. Asparouhova argues that people who support one but oppose the other reveal an inconsistent trust framework—trusting either government or corporations but not applying equal scrutiny to both. She contends that both governments and companies are run by fallible humans and deserve the same checks and balances. The core warning is that the tech world risks replacing one benevolent dictator (government) with another (corporations) if it doesn't demand accountability from both.
key_points: The Apple-FBI and Free Basics controversies reveal inconsistent attitudes toward power: people tend to trust either government or corporations but rarely scrutinize both equally.
Supporting Free Basics while opposing backdoors amounts to replacing faith in government with uncritical faith in tech companies—swapping one benevolent dictator for another.
Governments were never truly objective or neutral, as historical examples like the Three-Fifths Compromise demonstrate.
All power is subject to human error regardless of whether it resides in the public or private sector.
As technology reshapes governance, the goal should be creating checks and balances that apply to all concentrations of power.
money_quotes: But humans are humans, whether they work for the government or for a large tech company.
The private and public sector deserve equal scrutiny.
I believe all power is subject to human error.
To support Free Basics but oppose backdoors would be to commit the same fallacy as our predecessors. We are still putting faith in a benevolent dictator (companies) instead of in ourselves.
As we reimagine government in a world driven by technology, let's not accidentally replace one benevolent dictator with another.
Our goal should be to create a system of checks and balances that apply to everyone.
The government was never objective or neutral.
key_insight: Tech culture risks repeating history's mistakes by transferring uncritical trust from government to corporations, when both deserve equal scrutiny and checks on their power.
